entries.title: What is a Race Condition
entries.body:
A race condition occurs when a process's behavior critically and unexpectedly depends on the order or timing of other events. In a web application many requests are processed simultaneously, and developers often leave the question of concurrency to the framework, server, or language.

The essence of the attack: the application checks a condition and then does something based on it (the TOCTOU pattern - time-of-check to time-of-use). If you manage to send several requests so that they fall into this "window" between the check and the action, the check fires once, but the action - many times.

entries.title: Where to look for a race condition
entries.body:
Look where there is a limit check and an action tied to it over a shared resource:
• Limits on a single action: redeeming a gift card/coupon once, invitation limit, voting limit.
• Money and balance: debit/credit, withdrawal of funds, exceeding a limit (overdraw), spending one gift card multiple times.
• Anti-bruteforce and rate-limit: bypassing the limit on the number of attempts (login, OTP entry), 2FA bypass.
• Registration/invites: registering several users with one invitation.
• Anything "can only be done once / no more than N times" where the value is read and then overwritten.

entries.title: Limit-overrun
entries.body:
Limit-overrun is a scenario where several threads or processes compete to update/access a shared resource, as a result of which the resource goes beyond its intended limits.

Examples: overdrawing, multiple voting, spending a gift card multiple times.

Public reports:
• Race Condition allows to redeem multiple times gift cards which leads to free "money" - @muon4 (hackerone.com/reports/759247)
• Race conditions can be used to bypass invitation limit - @franjkovic (hackerone.com/reports/115007)
• Register multiple users using one invitation - @franjkovic (hackerone.com/reports/148609)

entries.title: Rate-limit Bypass
entries.body:
A rate-limit bypass happens when an attacker exploits the lack of proper synchronization in the rate-limiting mechanism to exceed the intended request limit. Rate-limiting is intended to control the frequency of actions (API requests, login attempts), but a race condition allows bypassing these restrictions.

Examples: bypassing an anti-bruteforce mechanism and 2FA.

Case: Instagram Password Reset Mechanism Race Condition - Laxman Muthiyah (youtu.be/4O9FjTMlHUM).

entries.title: HTTP/2 Single-packet Attack
entries.body:
In HTTP/2 you can send several HTTP requests in parallel over a single connection. In a single-packet attack, around ~20/30 requests are sent, and they all arrive at the server simultaneously. Using a single packet removes network jitter.

How to do it in Burp Suite:
• Send the request to Repeater
• Duplicate the request 20 times (CTRL+R)
• Create a new group and add all the requests to it
• Send group in parallel (single-packet attack)

Ready-made script: PortSwigger/turbo-intruder/race-single-packet-attack.py

Case: CVE-2022-4037 - Discovering a race condition vulnerability in Gitlab with the single-packet attack - James Kettle (youtu.be/Y0NVIVucQNE).

entries.title: HTTP/1.1 Last-byte Synchronization
entries.body: Send all requests except the last byte, then "release" each request by sending the final byte. This synchronizes the arrival of requests at the server over HTTP/1.1 when the single-packet attack is not available.

meta.tip: queue with the same gate puts the requests in a queue, holding back the last byte; openGate synchronously sends it to all requests. Case: Cracking reCAPTCHA, Turbo Intruder style - James Kettle (portswigger.net/research/cracking-recaptcha-turbo-intruder-style).

entries.title: Turbo Intruder - Example 1 (single endpoint)
entries.body:
def queueRequests(target, wordlists):
    engine = RequestEngine(endpoint=target.endpoint,
                        concurrentConnections=30,
                        requestsPerConnection=30,
                        pipeline=False
                        )

for i in range(30):
    engine.queue(target.req, i)
        engine.queue(target.req, target.baseInput, gate='race1')


    engine.start(timeout=5)
engine.openGate('race1')

    engine.complete(timeout=60)


def handleResponse(req, interesting):
    table.add(req)

meta.tip: Order of actions: 1) send the request to Turbo Intruder; 2) paste this python code as the payload; 3) set an external HTTP header x-request: %s (Turbo Intruder needs it); 4) click "Attack". The script is reproduced verbatim from PayloadsAllTheThings - its indentation is "broken", so before running, align the for/engine.start/openGate/complete blocks inside queueRequests.

entries.title: Turbo Intruder - Example 2 (two endpoints, request2 right after request1)
entries.body:
def queueRequests(target, wordlists):
    engine = RequestEngine(endpoint=target.endpoint,
                           concurrentConnections=30,
                           requestsPerConnection=100,
                           pipeline=False
                           )
    request1 = '''
POST /target-URI-1 HTTP/1.1
Host: <REDACTED>
Cookie: session=<REDACTED>

parameterName=parameterValue
    '''

    request2 = '''
GET /target-URI-2 HTTP/1.1
Host: <REDACTED>
Cookie: session=<REDACTED>
    '''

    engine.queue(request1, gate='race1')
    for i in range(30):
        engine.queue(request2, gate='race1')
    engine.openGate('race1')
    engine.complete(timeout=60)
def handleResponse(req, interesting):
    table.add(req)

meta.tip: A template for the case when request2 needs to be sent right after request1, and the window is only a few milliseconds. Substitute your own URIs, Host, and session cookie in place of <REDACTED>.

entries.title: Tools for race condition
entries.body:
• PortSwigger/turbo-intruder - a Burp Suite extension for sending a large number of HTTP requests and analyzing the results.
• JavanXD/Raceocat - makes exploiting race conditions in web applications efficient and convenient.
• nxenon/h2spacex - a low-level library/tool for the HTTP/2 Single Packet Attack based on Scapy + exploitation of timing attacks.
